title: Rectification and smoothing
summary: Learn how to convert alternating current (AC) from the mains supply into the steady direct current (DC) required by most electronic devices. This lesson covers rectification using diodes and smoothing using capacitors to produce a stable DC voltage.
learningObjectives: Describe the function of a semiconductor diode as a rectifier.
Distinguish between half-wave and full-wave rectification with reference to the corresponding circuits and output voltage graphs.
Explain the role of a smoothing capacitor connected in parallel with the load.
Analyse and sketch graphs showing the effect of rectification and smoothing on an alternating potential difference.

simpleExplanation.title: From Wavy AC to Steady DC
simpleExplanation.summary: Most gadgets need steady DC power, but our wall sockets provide wavy AC. We use electronic 'one-way streets' called diodes to stop the backward flow, and a 'mini-reservoir' called a capacitor to smooth out the remaining bumps.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine trying to fill a bucket using a pump that pushes water out then sucks it back in (AC). A one-way valve (a diode) stops the water from being sucked back, so you only get pulses of water going forward. If you add a small tank (a capacitor) after the valve, it stores water from each pulse and releases it steadily, creating a much smoother flow (DC).
steps: The input is a sinusoidal alternating voltage, switching between positive and negative polarity. This is the raw power from a transformer.

Diodes in a specific arrangement (e.g., a bridge rectifier) act like one-way gates, forcing the current to always flow in the same direction through the load. Negative half-cycles are either blocked (half-wave) or flipped to become positive (full-wave).
A capacitor is placed in parallel with the output load. It charges up to the peak voltage of the rectified signal.

As the rectified voltage drops from its peak, the capacitor discharges slowly through the load, maintaining the voltage. This significantly reduces the voltage variation, or 'ripple', creating a much steadier DC supply.

flashcards.back: The process of converting an alternating current (AC) or voltage into a direct current (DC) or voltage, which flows in only one direction.

flashcards.front: What is the key component used for rectification?
flashcards.back: A semiconductor diode, which allows current to flow easily in one direction (forward bias) but has a very high resistance in the opposite direction (reverse bias).

flashcards.front: Describe the output of a half-wave rectifier.
flashcards.back: For a sinusoidal AC input, the output consists of only the positive (or only the negative) half-cycles. The other half-cycles are blocked, resulting in zero voltage for half the time.

flashcards.front: Describe the output of a full-wave rectifier.
flashcards.back: For a sinusoidal AC input, the negative half-cycles are inverted to become positive. The output consists of a series of positive peaks with no zero-voltage gaps.
An arrangement of four diodes in a diamond-like configuration used for full-wave rectification. It is commonly used because it does not require a centre-tapped transformer.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of a smoothing capacitor?
flashcards.back: To reduce the variation (ripple) in the potential difference of a rectified output, making it closer to a steady DC voltage. It is always connected in parallel with the load.

flashcards.front: How does a smoothing capacitor work?
flashcards.back: It charges when the rectified voltage is near its peak and then discharges slowly through the load resistor when the rectified voltage falls, thus 'filling in' the troughs between peaks.
The small, periodic variation in potential difference that remains on a smoothed DC voltage. A smaller ripple voltage indicates better smoothing.

flashcards.front: How does the time constant, $\tau = RC$, affect smoothing?
flashcards.back: For effective smoothing, the time constant of the capacitor-resistor circuit must be much larger than the period of the rectified signal ($\tau \gg T$). This ensures the capacitor discharges slowly.

flashcards.front: What is a common exam trap when sketching a smoothed output?
flashcards.back: Showing the capacitor discharge as a straight line. It must be an exponential decay curve. Also, the smoothed voltage should not touch the time axis after the initial rise.

flashcards.front: Why is the peak output voltage of a real rectifier slightly less than the peak input voltage?
flashcards.back: Because of the forward bias voltage drop (typically ~0.7V) across each conducting diode. In a bridge rectifier, two diodes are always conducting in series, so the drop is ~1.4V.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between the input AC frequency and the ripple frequency for a full-wave rectifier?
flashcards.back: The ripple frequency of the output from a full-wave rectifier is double the frequency of the input AC supply. For a 50 Hz input, the ripple frequency is 100 Hz.

faq.q: Why is full-wave rectification more efficient than half-wave?
faq.a: Full-wave rectification utilises both the positive and negative half-cycles of the AC input, converting all the input power into a unidirectional output. Half-wave rectification blocks the negative half-cycles entirely, effectively discarding half of the available power. This makes full-wave rectification twice as efficient in terms of power transfer.

faq.q: What happens to the smoothing if I use a smaller load resistor (e.g., a more power-hungry device)?
faq.a: A smaller load resistance $R$ will decrease the time constant of the smoothing circuit ($\tau = RC$). This means the capacitor will discharge more quickly through the load between rectified peaks. As a result, the voltage will drop more significantly, leading to a larger ripple voltage and less effective smoothing. To maintain good smoothing for a smaller load resistance, a larger capacitance $C$ would be required.

faq.q: Why use a bridge of four diodes for full-wave rectification instead of just two?
faq.a: A full-wave rectifier can be made with two diodes, but it requires a centre-tapped transformer. This special transformer has an extra connection at the midpoint of its secondary coil, effectively creating two separate AC outputs that are 180° out of phase. The four-diode bridge rectifier is more common because it can be used with a simpler, standard transformer that does not have a centre tap, making the overall power supply design more versatile and often cheaper.

sections.content: Many of our essential electronic devices, from laptops to smartphones, require a steady direct current (DC) to function. However, the mains electricity supplied to our homes is alternating current (AC). This topic explores the crucial process of converting AC to DC, a fundamental concept in electronics. We will investigate how diodes can 'rectify' the AC signal and how capacitors can 'smooth' it into a usable DC supply.
Rectification: Forcing Current Down a One-Way Street

Rectification is the process of converting AC into DC. The essential component for this task is the semiconductor diode. A diode behaves like a one-way valve for electric current. It has a very low resistance when current flows in the 'forward' direction (forward bias) and a very high resistance when current tries to flow in the 'reverse' direction (reverse bias). This property allows us to block or redirect parts of the AC cycle.

The simplest form of rectification uses a single diode in series with the load resistor. During the positive half-cycles of the AC input, the diode is forward-biased and conducts, allowing current to flow through the load. During the negative half-cycles, the diode is reverse-biased and does not conduct, blocking the current. The result is an output voltage that consists only of the positive half-cycles of the original AC waveform, with gaps of zero voltage in between. While simple, this method is inefficient as it discards half of the input power.

A more efficient method is full-wave rectification, which utilises the entire AC waveform. The most common circuit for this is the bridge rectifier, which uses four diodes arranged in a specific configuration. During the positive half-cycle of the input, one pair of diodes is forward-biased, directing current through the load. During the negative half-cycle, the other pair of diodes becomes forward-biased, again directing current through the load *in the same direction*. This inverts the negative half-cycles, resulting in a continuous series of positive peaks. This method is more powerful and provides an output that is easier to smooth.

items: **Half-Wave:** Uses one diode, blocks 50% of the AC cycle, inefficient.
**Full-Wave:** Uses four diodes (in a bridge), inverts the negative half-cycle, utilises 100% of the AC cycle, more efficient.
**Output Frequency:** For half-wave, the output frequency equals the input frequency. For full-wave, the output frequency is double the input frequency.

sections.question:
An AC supply with a peak voltage of 9.0 V and frequency of 50 Hz is connected to a full-wave bridge rectifier. Each diode has a forward bias voltage drop of 0.7 V.

(a) Determine the peak voltage of the rectified output.
(b) What is the frequency of the ripple in the rectified output?
sections.solution:
(a) In a bridge rectifier, the current always passes through two diodes in series for any part of the AC cycle. The total voltage drop across these two diodes must be subtracted from the input voltage.
Total voltage drop = $2 \times 0.7 \text{ V} = 1.4 \text{ V}$.
The peak output voltage is the peak input voltage minus this drop.
$V_{peak, out} = V_{peak, in} - 1.4 \text{ V}$
$V_{peak, out} = 9.0 \text{ V} - 1.4 \text{ V} = 7.6 \text{ V}$.

(b) The input frequency is 50 Hz. Full-wave rectification uses both the positive and negative half-cycles of the input to create positive peaks in the output. This means there are two output peaks for every one input cycle.
Therefore, the frequency of the output signal (the ripple frequency) is double the input frequency.
Ripple frequency = $2 \times 50 \text{ Hz} = 100 \text{ Hz}$.

sections.content: Smoothing the Output with a Capacitor

The output from a rectifier is unidirectional but not constant; it's a series of pulses. For most electronic circuits, a steady DC voltage is required. This is achieved by smoothing. A large capacitor is connected in parallel with the load resistor. The capacitor acts as a temporary energy store.

As the rectified voltage rises towards its peak, the capacitor charges up. When the rectified voltage begins to fall after the peak, the capacitor's potential difference is momentarily higher than the supply's. The diode(s) become reverse-biased, and the capacitor begins to discharge its stored energy through the load resistor. This discharge maintains the voltage across the load, 'filling in' the gap until the next voltage peak from the rectifier recharges it. The result is a much steadier DC voltage with only a small variation, known as ripple.
The rate of discharge is determined by the time constant of the capacitor-resistor circuit: $$\tau = RC$$

items: For effective smoothing, the time constant $\tau$ must be much larger than the period $T$ of the rectified signal ($\tau \gg T$).
A large time constant means the capacitor discharges very slowly, resulting in a small ripple voltage and a smoother output.
This can be achieved by using a large capacitance $C$ and/or a large load resistance $R$.

sections.content: When sketching graphs of smoothed, rectified voltage, be precise. The voltage should rise quickly with the rectified signal to its peak. The discharge phase must be shown as a curve (exponential decay), not a straight line. The smoothed voltage should never fall to zero. Also, remember that the ripple frequency for a full-wave rectifier is twice the mains frequency.

sections.question:
A smoothing capacitor of 4700 µF is connected in parallel with a load resistor. This circuit is connected to a 50 Hz full-wave rectifier with a peak output voltage of 12 V. The resulting ripple voltage is not to exceed 0.5 V.

(a) What is the period of the rectified waveform?
(b) Estimate the minimum value of the load resistance required to achieve this level of smoothing.
sections.solution:
(a) The input frequency is 50 Hz. For a full-wave rectifier, the output frequency is doubled.
$f_{out} = 2 \times 50 \text{ Hz} = 100 \text{ Hz}$.
The period $T$ of the rectified waveform is the reciprocal of this frequency.
$T = 1 / f_{out} = 1 / 100 = 0.010 \text{ s}$ or 10 ms.

(b) We can estimate the ripple voltage $\Delta V$ using the approximation that the capacitor discharges for a time $\Delta t \approx T$. The current $I$ during discharge is approximately $V_0 / R$, where $V_0$ is the peak voltage.
The charge lost is $\Delta Q = I \Delta t \approx (V_0 / R) T$. 
The change in voltage is $\Delta V = \Delta Q / C$.
So, $\Delta V \approx (V_0 T) / (RC)$.
We need to find $R$ for a given $\Delta V$. Rearranging the formula:
$R \approx (V_0 T) / (\Delta V C)$
Substitute the given values:
$V_0 = 12 \text{ V}$
$T = 0.010 \text{ s}$
$\Delta V = 0.5 \text{ V}$
$C = 4700 \text{ µF} = 4700 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}$
$R \approx (12 \times 0.010) / (0.5 \times 4700 \times 10^{-6})$
$R \approx 0.12 / (2.35 \times 10^{-3})$
$R \approx 51.1 \text{ } \Omega$.
Therefore, the load resistance must be at least 51 Ω.
